retrieved_docs.title: Prostaglandins -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Misoprostol's mechanism of action has led to several off-label uses in obstetrics and gynecology. [25] The drug selectively binds to EP2 and EP3 receptors, leading to uterine muscle contractions. As a result, it is used for cervical ripening and labor induction. [25] As misoprostol induces uterine contractions, it is also used for the termination of intrauterine pregnancy in the first trimester and for the treatment of postpartum hemorrhage. [26] [14] Carboprost, a PGF2α analog, has potent uterotonic effects, compresses blood vessels, and stops bleeding. [6] Dinoprostone, a PGE2 analog, stimulates the release of PGF2α, thereby increasing the sensitivity of uterine muscles to both endogenous and exogenous oxytocin. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostaglandins -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandin analogs are synthetic compounds that mimic the effects of naturally occurring prostaglandins and are used for a wide range of clinical indications. They are used across multiple specialties, including ophthalmology, obstetrics, urology, gastroenterology, cardiology, and pulmonology. FDA-approved indications include PGF2α analogs (latanoprost, bimatoprost, travoprost, tafluprost, and latanoprostene bunod) for glaucoma; bimatoprost for eyelash hypotrichosis; PGE1 analogs (misoprostol and alprostadil) for NSAID-induced gastric ulcers, erectile dysfunction, and patent ductus arteriosus; PGF2α analog carboprost for postpartum hemorrhage and abortion induction; PGE2 analog dinoprostone for labor induction; and PGI2 analogs (epoprostenol, treprostinil, and iloprost) for pulmonary arterial hypertension, with iloprost also approved for severe frostbite. Misoprostol is also used off-label for labor induction, cervical ripening, pregnancy termination, and postpartum hemorrhage.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Prostaglandin I2 -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandin I2 plays many roles in the body, but its primary known functions are to inhibit platelet aggregation, affect smooth muscle relaxation and vasodilation, and limit immune cell proliferation. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Prostaglandin I2 -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandin I2 also provides potent vasodilatory effects by acting on smooth muscle as a relaxant. COX-2 and its corresponding PGI-Synthase are upregulated in hypoxia to promote a vasodilatory response. [16] These receptors are found in increased levels in the pulmonary vasculature and are suspected of serving a protective role therein. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostaglandin E2 (Dinoprostone) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: While the exact mechanism of action is unknown, prostaglandin E2 causes contractions in the myometrium via direct stimulation.  It binds to G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) EP1-4 that lead to a variety of downstream events depending on the EP subtype and cell-type-specific expression patterns. [3] For example, EP receptors in the myometrium act via cell membrane calcium channels and intracellular cyclic 3’5’-adenosine monophosphate (cAMP). As some of the known receptors for prostaglandin E2 antagonize each other, researchers have hypothesized that the expression of these receptors determines the specific effects. The efficacy of prostaglandin E2 during pregnancy may link to the expression of these receptors. Prostaglandin E2 also promotes cervical dilation, effacement, and softening, similar to the natural progression of pregnancy, possibly due to increased collagenase secretion. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostaglandin E1(Archived) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: In the treatment of erectile dysfunction, prostaglandin E1 (PGE1) works by increasing cAMP production, reducing the influx of calcium within the penile vascular smooth muscle. The result is a relaxation of trabecular smooth muscle by arterial dilation. Blood becomes entrapped in the penis, which eventually leads to an erection. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostaglandin E1(Archived) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: PGE1 can also serve as a treatment for maintaining the patency of ductus arteriosus heart defects in neonates by a similar mechanism. PGE1 dilates the ductus smooth muscle so that it remains open and blood flow may be maintained. Ductal patency allows for oxygenated blood flow until the congenital anomaly can be surgically corrected. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Alprostadil -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Alprostadil is a synthetic analog of PGE1 with various pharmacologic effects. This drug binds as an agonist to prostaglandin receptors (eg, EP2), thereby activating adenylate cyclase. This results in an accumulation of 3'5'-cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP), which is responsible for the pharmacological effects of the medication, including smooth muscle relaxation that leads to vasodilation (increasing peripheral and intracavernosal blood flow), bronchodilation, and inhibition of platelet aggregation. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Indomethacin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) relaxes smooth muscle and inhibits the closure of the ductus arteriosus. In preterm infants with respiratory distress syndrome, the ductus arteriosus fails to close, resulting in patent ductus arteriosus due to the relatively high concentration of PGE2. Uncorrected patent ductus arteriosus can lead to differential cyanosis. Indomethacin aids in closing a patent ductus arteriosus by inhibiting the synthesis of PGE2. [1] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Prostaglandin I2 -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacologic administration of PGI2 analogs is useful in pulmonary arterial hypertension, peripheral occlusive disease, and diabetic vascular complications. [3] Epoprostenol is a synthetic prostacyclin that can be given through parenteral administration and causes preferential vasodilation in the pulmonary vasculature, though effects occur in all vasculature. [17] Additionally, several animal models have shown promising results for the use of inhaled iloprost in the treatment for asthma due to its immunomodulatory effects. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Prostaglandin I2 -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandin I2 is synthesized via COX-2 and PGIS from membrane phospholipids upon pro-inflammatory stimulation via cytokines, growth factors, or other physical and chemical exogenous stimuli. [3] [4] PGI is primarily synthesized in vascular endothelial cells and smooth muscle cells but is also synthesized in fibroblasts, follicular dendritic cells, and thymic nurse cells. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostaglandins -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Alprostadil relaxes smooth muscles in the corpus cavernosum and dilates the cavernous arteries, producing an erection. [27] Alprostadil also relaxes the smooth muscles of the ductus arteriosus in neonates. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostaglandins -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Zeng et al identified FDA-approved and off-label indications for various prostaglandin analogs based on their receptor subtypes and mechanisms of action. [11] In addition, PGI2 inhibits platelet aggregation, reduces inflammation, and limits smooth muscle proliferation.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Prostaglandin I2 -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandins play an essential role in the closure of prenatal cardiac shunts [6] . Though not as significant as prostaglandin E2, prostaglandin I2 levels rise in preparation for birth. [6] Neonates born preterm also show reduced levels of prostanoids; this theoretically contributes to the increased rates of congenital defects such as patent ductus arteriosus in preterm infants. [7] Additionally, low dose prostaglandins can help to induce labor, particularly in overdue pregnancies. [8] Prostaglandin E2 and I2 are natural vascular relaxants, and inhibitors of their expression are useful as therapy to close patent ductus arteriosus in neonates. [7] The prostaglandin synthase inhibitor indomethacin or ibuprofen are commonly used for the treatment of symptomatic patent ductus artreriosus in preterm infants. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Prostaglandin I2 -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandin I2 and the COX-2 system is found widely in the body, so the effects of regulation are diverse, and many have not yet been adequately studied. [3] However, a few places of note are in pulmonary and cardiac vasculature, peripheral vasculature, and various immune cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Prostaglandin I2 -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1877
retrieved_docs.content: The diclofenac / misoprostol oral product contains misoprostol as one of its active ingredients. Misoprostol is a synthetic analogue that is derived from prostaglandin A, placing it within the prostaglandin family of compounds. This parent‑child relationship explains misoprostol’s pharmacologic actions, such as enhancing gastric mucosal protection and inducing uterine contractility. Consequently, the inclusion of misoprostol in the diclofenac / misoprostol combination contributes to the product’s ability to mitigate the gastrointestinal adverse effects commonly associated with diclofenac, an NSAID, while providing additional therapeutic effects linked to its prostaglandin‑derived activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2337
retrieved_docs.content: Dinoproston, also known as prostaglandin E₂, is classified as a member of the prostaglandin oxytocics group. Prostaglandin oxytocics are a class of prostaglandin compounds that stimulate uterine contractions and are commonly employed in obstetric practice for cervical ripening, induction of labor, and medical termination of pregnancy. By belonging to this class, dinoproston shares these pharmacologic properties and is used clinically to facilitate labor and manage certain gynecologic conditions, guiding clinicians in the selection and expected effects of uterotonic therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 172
retrieved_docs.content: by the broader category “Prostaglandin Analog [EPC].” Together, these relationships illustrate how measurement techniques (including ++Mg assessment, cardiac stress testing, and arterial blood‑gas analysis) intersect with the biochemical synthesis of prostaglandins via cyclooxygenase and their diverse clinical applications through numerous prostaglandin analogues in ophthalmology and obstetric‑gynecologic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 946
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandiny, representing the family of prostaglandins, are biologically active lipid compounds that are measured in clinical prostaglandin measurement assays as a key component. They belong to the broader eicosanoid class, reflecting their origin from arachidonic acid metabolism, and are closely linked to the cyclooxygenase (COX) enzymatic pathway, which governs their synthesis. In therapeutic practice, prostaglandiny serve as the parent class for several drug groups that are primarily employed in obstetrics, gynecology, and urinary‑tract disorders, underscoring their importance in managing conditions such as labor induction, cervical ripening, and bladder dysfunction. Specific pharmaceutical agents—including epoprostenol, isopropyl unoprostone, and the Prostaglandin Analog [EPC]—are classified within the structural class of prostaglandiny, indicating that they are designed to mimic or modify the activity of natural prostaglandins. These analogs are used to treat pulmonary hypertension, glaucoma, and other conditions where modulation of prostaglandin pathways is beneficial. Together, the measurement, biochemical relationships, and therapeutic applications of prostaglandiny illustrate their central role in both physiological regulation and targeted medical interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2713
retrieved_docs.content: Dinoprost is classified as a member of the prostaglandin oxytocic group, a class of agents that act as uterotonic drugs to stimulate uterine smooth‑muscle contraction. This categorization indicates that dinoprost shares the pharmacologic properties of prostaglandin oxytocics, which are commonly used to induce labor, augment uterine activity during delivery, and manage postpartum hemorrhage. Understanding dinoprost’s membership in this drug class helps clinicians anticipate its mechanism of action, therapeutic indications, and potential side‑effects related to uterine stimulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2624
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandin Analog [EPC] is classified within the prostaglandin structural class, indicating that it shares the core chemical framework of prostaglandins. Epoprostenol is described as a “child” of prostaglandin, meaning it is a derivative that also belongs to this prostaglandin class. Isopropyl unoprostone and travoprost are likewise assigned to the prostaglandin structural class, linking these agents together as prostaglandin analogs. The enzyme cyklooxygenasy (cyclooxygenase) is noted to have a relationship with prostaglandin, reflecting its central role in the biosynthesis of prostaglandin compounds. Collectively, these connections illustrate that several ocular hypotensive and vasodilatory drugs—Prostaglandin Analog [EPC], epoprostenol, isopropyl unoprostone, and travoprost—share a common prostaglandin‑based chemical architecture, while cyclooxygenase is a key enzyme associated with prostaglandin metabolism, a relationship that underlies the pharmacologic actions and therapeutic considerations of these medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 650
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandins (prostaginy) are biologically active lipid mediators that serve as key components in prostaglandin measurement assays, where their levels are quantified for diagnostic purposes. They are classified within the broader group of hormones, their metabolites, and precursors, underscoring their role as hormone‑like signaling molecules. Prostaglandins also belong to the eicosanoid family (ikosanoidy), representing a specific subclass of these polyunsaturated fatty‑acid derivatives. The enzyme cyclooxygenase (cyklooxygenasy) is directly linked to prostaglandin biosynthesis, establishing a functional relationship between the enzyme and prostaglandin production. Several therapeutic drug groups primarily used in obstetrics, gynecology, and urinary tract disorders are derived from or based on prostaglandins, reflecting their clinical utility in managing uterine contractility, cervical ripening, and smooth‑muscle tone. Isopropyl unoprostone is identified as having a structural class of prostaglandins, exemplifying a synthetic analog that mimics prostaglandin activity. Additionally, prostaglandin endoperoxides (prostaglandinové endoperoxidy) are recognized as downstream products—or “children”—of prostaglandins within the metabolic pathway. Collectively, these interconnections highlight the central role of prostaglandins in physiology, pharmacology, and diagnostic testing.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1097
retrieved_docs.content: Platelet aggregation inhibitors excl. heparin is a pharmacologic class that encompasses a diverse group of agents used to prevent platelet clumping without employing heparin. Members of this class include the prostacyclin analogues beraprost, epoprostenol, iloprost and treprostinil, which exert antiplatelet effects by mimicking prostacyclin’s vasodilatory and platelet‑inhibiting actions. The oral P2Y12 receptor antagonists clopidogrel (noted as “klopidogrel” in the source) and ticagrelor also belong to this group, inhibiting ADP‑mediated platelet activation through distinct binding sites on the receptor. Selexipag, a selective prostacyclin IP‑receptor agonist, is likewise classified as a platelet aggregation inhibitor excl. heparin. Together, these drugs provide multiple mechanistic options for reducing platelet aggregation in clinical settings where heparin is contraindicated or undesirable.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1990
retrieved_docs.content: Diclofenac Sodium/Misoprostol is a combination medication that incorporates misoprostol as a constituent component, and the oral dosage formulation marketed as “Diclofenac and misoprostol only product in oral dose form” lists misoprostol among its active ingredients. Misoprostol itself is a synthetic analogue derived from prostaglandin A, indicating that its pharmacologic activity is based on the prostaglandin family. Together, these relationships clarify that the therapeutic effects of the Diclofenac‑misoprostol combination are mediated in part by misoprostol, whose origin as a prostaglandin A derivative underlies its mechanism of action.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 673
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandin measurement incorporates prostaglandins as a key component, reflecting the central role of these lipid mediators in diagnostic testing. Drug groups primarily used in obstetrics, gynaecology and urinary tract disorders are classified under a parent category of prostaglandins, indicating that many agents in these specialties are prostaglandin‑based or act on prostaglandin pathways. Prostaglandin cardiac therapy preparations are linked to prostaglandins through a broader therapeutic relationship, as several cardiac drugs are prostaglandin analogues that modulate vascular tone and platelet function. Epoprostenol is identified as a child of prostaglandins, representing a specific synthetic prostacyclin analogue employed in the treatment of pulmonary hypertension. Prostanoids, also known as eicosanoids, are the larger family to which prostaglandins belong, situating prostaglandins as a subclass within the eicosanoid lipid mediator system. Finally, isopropyl unoprostone is described as having a structural class of prostaglandins, underscoring its chemical similarity to natural prostaglandins and its use in ophthalmic therapy. Together, these relationships illustrate how prostaglandins serve as a foundational biochemical and pharmacological concept across measurement techniques, therapeutic drug groups, cardiac and pulmonary treatments, and ocular medications, all within the broader eicosanoid family.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1307
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandins are a subclass of eicosanoids that play a central role in many physiological and pathological processes. The enzyme cyclooxygenase is closely related to prostaglandins because it catalyzes their biosynthesis from arachidonic acid. Several therapeutic agents are structurally classified as prostaglandins, including the drug group primarily used in obstetrics, gynaecology and urinary tract disorders, Prostaglandin Analog [EPC], bimatoprost, and isopropyl unoprostone. Recognizing that these medications share a prostaglandin structural class and that prostaglandins themselves belong to the broader eicosanoid family helps clinicians understand their mechanisms of action, potential side‑effects, and appropriate clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 1697
retrieved_docs.content: The “Diclofenac and misoprostol only product” formulation lists misoprostol as its active ingredient, indicating that misoprostol is the therapeutic component responsible for the product’s pharmacologic effect. Similarly, the combination tablet identified as “diclofenac sodium 75 MG / misoprostol 0.2 MG Delayed Release Oral Tablet” includes misoprostol among its ingredients, demonstrating that misoprostol is incorporated into multi‑drug regimens that also contain diclofenac sodium. Chemically, misoprostol is derived from prostaglandiny A, making it a synthetic prostaglandin analog; this relationship explains its mechanism of action and its use in gastrointestinal protection and other clinical indications. Together, these connections illustrate how misoprostol functions both as an active pharmaceutical ingredient in specific diclofenac‑containing products and as a prostaglandin‑based compound.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1313
retrieved_docs.content: The oral dosage form identified as “Diclofenac and misoprostol only product in oral dose form” contains misoprostol as an active ingredient, and the combination referred to as “diclofenac / misoprostol” likewise includes misoprostol within its composition. Similarly, the “diclofenac / misoprostol Oral Product” lists misoprostol among its active components. Chemically, misoprostol is derived from prostaglandin A, indicating that it is a prostaglandin analog. Understanding that misoprostol is the active prostaglandin‑derived constituent of these oral diclofenac‑misoprostol products informs clinicians about the pharmacologic basis of the medication and its potential effects related to prostaglandin pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 31
retrieved_docs.content: not intersect with the prostaglandin network. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how prostaglandins function as a unifying biochemical and pharmacological framework that connects hormone metabolism, obstetric‑gynaecologic therapies, ocular pressure‑lowering drugs, cardiac treatments and downstream drug families, thereby guiding clinicians in selecting agents with shared structural and functional properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1354
retrieved_docs.content: The pharmaceutical preparations identified as “Diclofenac and misoprostol only product” and “Diclofenac and misoprostol only product in oral dose form” both list misoprostol as an active ingredient. In addition, the combination formulation referred to as “diclofenac / misoprostol” also contains misoprostol. Misoprostol is chemically derived from prostaglandiny A, making it a prostaglandin A analogue. These connections illustrate that misoprostol, a prostaglandin‑derived agent, is a central component of several diclofenac‑misoprostol combination products, which may be relevant for therapeutic strategies that utilize its pharmacologic properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1382
retrieved_docs.content: The oral tablet diclofenac sodium 50 MG / misoprostol 0.2 MG Delayed Release Oral Tablet, the Diclofenac and misoprostol only product, and the diclofenac / misoprostol Oral Product all contain diclofenac as their active moiety or active ingredient. Each of these formulations combines diclofenac, a non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drug, with misoprostol, a prostaglandin analogue, to provide anti‑inflammatory efficacy while mitigating gastrointestinal toxicity. Recognizing that diclofenac is the principal active component across these products is essential for appropriate prescribing, monitoring for NSAID‑related adverse effects, and delivering accurate patient counseling.
